news.title: Post-war Hormuz escort mission takes shape, despite Trump-Macron row
news.text:
France and the UK are preparing to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when the Iran war is over, but the French president has risked igniting a personal row with US leader Donald Trump.

The UK foreign ministry hosted diplomats from “over 40” countries in London on Thursday (2 April) to discuss the Hormuz operation, with foreign secretary Yvette Cooper saying Iran had no right to “hijack an international shipping route to hold the global economy hostage”.

Iran has hit 23 commercial ships since the US and Israel attacked it on 28 February, killing 11 crew, according to British maritime journal Lloyd’s List, while a further 2,000 or so ships were stranded in the region with 20,000 sailors on board.

A Franco-British-led mission, including minesweepers, would restore confidence for ships to go through, while skirting closer to the Omani coast than before, driving down insurance costs and oil prices.

It would also counter Trump’s tirades about leaving Nato, by half-meeting his demands for the EU to send its navies to help.

But French president Emmanuel Macron again ruled out involvement until the war is over.

“There are those who advocate for the liberation of the Strait of Hormuz by force through a military operation,” he told press, while visiting Seoul on Thursday.

“It is unrealistic because it would take an inordinate amount of time and would expose anyone crossing the strait to coastal threats from the [Iranian] Revolutionary Guards, who possess significant resources, as well as ballistic missiles, [and] a host of other risks,” he said.

And military experts, such as retired Italian admiral Giampaolo Di Paola, agreed.

“At present, with the war ongoing the risk is too much and a European presence now would be seen by Iran as getting involved on the side of the US,” he told EUobserver on Thursday.

But a post-war mission would be “perfectly feasible”, he said, also because the “geopolitical landscape” would change.

“Now Iran, somehow, may have some sort of sympathy, some understanding because they have been attacked and are trying to defend themselves,” Di Paola said.

But Hormuz was “not Iran’s property” and was meant to stay open under the law of the sea, he added, meaning that the UN Security Council would likely give its blessing to an international mission.

“At that point, if they attacked a French or Italian boat, it’s Iran against the world, not Iran against the US,” he said.

And it could happen soon, as Trump was looking for an off-ramp, due to lack of political support at home for a full invasion of Iran.

“He’s looking for a way out. He’ll say: ‘We destroyed their nuclear capability, we destroyed their military, and there has been regime change … as Khamenei is dead, but as for Hormuz – it’s up to you [Europe]’,” Di Paola said, referring to the assassinated Iranian supreme leader.
news.author: Andrew Rettman

news.title: 10 days to election: Campaign in Hungary resembles political spy movie
news.text:
In our ongoing weekly digest, award-winning journalist Veronika Munk summarises and explains the most important political developments in Hungary.

We guide readers on the route to the crunch 12 April 2026 election — a poll without precedent in the region over the past 15 years. This week, we cover:

According the Fidesz government, almost anyone who opposes them is now a Ukrainian spy.

Opinion polls still show the opposition Tisza in the lead, and a three-party parliament may emerge.

Hard-right Mi Hazánk may hold the balance of power and could even support a minority Fidesz government, without joining it.

Péter Magyar is preparing for a two-thirds parliamentary mandate.

On Viktor Orbán’s nationwide tour, there are increasing numberes of protesters — with whom he is getting into open conflict.

Organised and coordinated groups are obstructing the Orbán protesters.

Follow Hungary’s road to the 2026 election — subscribe to Days Until the Hungarian Elections on Substack and get every edition, and other Hungary-related articles straight to your inbox.

Hungarian domestic politics really does now look like a spy movie

Excerpts from the past week, in chronological order:

A video is posted on the official Facebook page of the Hungarian government. The video is edited, with the sound bleeped out in several places, but it is clear that it was recorded in the interrogation room of the counter-intelligence agency, the Constitutional Protection Office [Alkotmányvédelmi Hivatal], in connection with an ongoing case, and that Tisza’s former 19‑year‑old IT specialist is being questioned. Later, state propaganda accuses him of having been targeted for recruitment by the Ukrainians, and then the entire pro-government media runs with the story that the teenager is a Ukrainian spy. He refutes this in an interview with 444.

The whole country listens to the Hungarian foreign minister fawning over the Russian foreign minister and promising that he will attempt, together with the Slovaks, to have the sister of a Russian oligarch removed from the EU sanctions lists. “I am always at your disposal,” Péter Szijjártó tells Sergei Lavrov in the 2024 audio recording. Seven months after the conversation, the woman was indeed removed from the EU sanctions list. Szijjártó’s conversations with Russian government officials were obtained and authenticated by a consortium of investigative newspapers.

At Thursday’s government press conference, minister Gergely Gulyás refers at least six times to investigative journalist Szabolcs Panyi as, in his view, a spy.

Quick take: Fidesz’s campaign strategy, built on war-scare tactics and anti-Ukrainian sentiment, has shifted into top gear. In this narrative, anyone who does not support the governing party is pro‑Ukrainian, or even a Ukrainian spy. With this, the government is riding a social trend according to which — presumably not least because of the Fidesz smear campaign — rejection of Ukraine and Ukrainians is indeed strengthening in the country. A study by Policy Solutions last year already showed that while in 2023 some 80 percent of the population supported the European Union’s humanitarian assistance to Ukraine, by 2025 this proportion had fallen to 63 percent, and three-quarters of respondents now oppose financial support from the Hungarian budget. The drastic decline in solidarity is particularly striking among pro-government voters.

Poll of the week: three-party parliament

According to the latest late‑March survey by Závecz Research, the Tisza party leads the race among decided party voters by 11 percentage points ahead of Fidesz. In the total population, Tisza stands at 39 percent and the governing party at 31 percent, which in absolute numbers means that while Orbán’s camp has roughly 2.4 million supporters, the largest opposition force already has a base of three million. The research indicates that willingness to vote is high (76 percent), but besides Tisza and Fidesz, at present only Mi Hazánk (five percent) would clear the parliamentary threshold among the group of decided voters.

According to fresh data from the 21 Research Centre [21 Kutatóközpont], Mi Hazánk is also the only smaller party that has a chance of crossing the five-percent parliamentary threshold. Mi Hazánk pursues a sovereigntist, EU‑sceptic and vaccine‑critical rhetoric. Although, at least verbally, both Fidesz and Mi Hazánk have ruled out the possibility of a future coalition, the small party could even support a minority Fidesz government from the outside, thereby preventing a political crisis.

The other two micro-parties still worth mentioning, the DK and the Two‑Tailed Dog Party, are very unlikely to enter parliament. Their candidates have started to withdraw, even though the DK leadership has forbidden them to do so.

Péter Magyar is preparing for two-thirds governing power

In the final sprint of the campaign, the Tisza leader is now speaking at six venues a day. In an interview with Partizán channel, he said he is consciously striving for a two‑thirds mandate. In his view, a two‑thirds majority is needed because then he can:

amend the Fundamental Law, among other things in order to cap the prime minister’s term at two cycles (a maximum of eight years);

dismantle the current system, which he describes as a “monolithic bloc” and a “captured state”;

remove, as he puts it, the Orbán‑puppet president of the republic, as well as the heads of the Competition Authority and the Data Protection Authority.

He also explained that his goal is not to create a “Fidesz-Lite” or an “Viktor Orbán 2” system, and pledges not to abuse a two‑thirds mandate.

Orbán snaps at young people: ‘you could at least say thank you’

In the town of Szentes, in south-east Hungary, Orbán got into a verbal altercation with young people protesting against him, who disrupted his speech with whistling, honking and chants of “Dirty Fidesz” and “Gang of Thieves”. Reacting to the heckling, the PM hit back at the demonstrators in a barbed tone: “I know the truth hurts, but sometimes you have to listen to it.” He then launched into a long list of benefits for young people, stressing student loans, job‑creation loans and tax breaks, which he said help them to start their lives.

In his speech, the head of government claimed that these days it is easiest to buy a home in Hungary in the whole of Europe, while in the crowd more and more people joined the group holding up a banner reading “It’s Over”. Orbán stressed that the government “has done everything, even in the shadow of war” for young people, and that they “could at least say thank you for the support they receive — not to the government, but to the others,” referring to the older generations, who are not entitled to similar tax breaks.

Muscular men-in-black obstruct counter-protesters on Orbán’s nationwide tour

In the final stretch of the campaign, Orbán has embarked on a nationwide tour — a new role for him. He has not met voters up close for a long time, and in recent years he has mostly appeared only before tightly-controlled crowds of supporters. Now, however, he has to face demonstrators from time to time during the tour, and in parallel with this, a familiar sight has emerged: a group of typically muscular men in black mingling with the crowd.

They are not counter‑protesters, but rather are organised in an effort to silence the demonstrators. Their activities follow a similar strategy everywhere and are based on physical superiority: they block the way of the protesters so they cannot get close to Orbán’s stage, they loudly chant slogans praising Orbán and Fidesz so that the hecklers cannot be heard, they cover up the banners, and sometimes they even tear the placards out of the protesters’ hands.

The leaders of these groups are not local activists, but government‑adjacent figures who follow the prime minister to every stop. Among them are the former head of a drug‑research institute endowed with public money, as well as a ministerial commissioner, a European Championships silver‑medal wrestler, football ultras, army reservists and a model.

So far no one has uncovered exactly from what sources and on whose orders these heavies protect Orbán’s stage, or why they seek to silence Orbán‑critical voices. What is certain, however, is that their method is nothing new. In Ukraine it was known as the “Titushki method”, after Ukrainian MMA fighter Vadym Titushko, who in 2013 in Kyiv, during Euromaidan, attacked journalists and opposition protesters demonstrating against Viktor Yanukovych’s autocratic policies.

In Hungary the level of aggression is far lower, but the method is similar: they seek to break critical voices and resistance, to create tension and to try to compromise opposition actions. A common feature is that they operate in groups, in a coordinated and disciplined manner. At first they fled and turned away from the cameras, but after more and more of them were identified by the press, they proudly posed for a group photo.

Solidarity in Hungary

Last week we wrote about former police officer Bence Szabó, who in recent days has become not only one of the main protagonists of the election campaign, but also one of the key figures standing up against the Orbán system that has been in place for 16 years.

Szabó was a senior investigator at the cybercrime department of the National Bureau of Investigation, and he appeared on camera, with his face and name, for the journalists of Direkt36 to recount how, in his view, the intelligence services sought to use their investigation to try to cripple the Tisza party.

Since Szabó’s interview, several former police officers and intelligence agents have reported experiencing political pressure in the course of their work.

After Szabó became unemployed, his brother launched a fundraising campaign for him so that he could repay in one lump sum the employer loan that has now been called back, and have resources for the legal proceedings. His brother aimed to raise €12,500 for him, but in the end donors contributed €729,482 (more than 280 million forints).

Szabó announced that he would distribute most of the money among civil society organisations, and would also give a share to his former school and kindergarten.
news.author: Veronika Munk

news.title: Risk of death or injury in Russian regiment almost 100% (Ukraine Battlefield update, Day 1,498)
news.text:
Every day, the Ukraine Battlefield update newsletter offers a clear look at how the war is unfolding on the ground, highlighting key developments along the frontline and the shifting dynamics of the conflict. This offers readers regular and detailed information to better understand the implications of the war for the country and the whole continent.

A leaked document indicates the losses suffered by Russia’s 27th Guards Division. 

For newly recruited soldiers, the risk of death or injury was close to 100 percent. 

The Ukrainian Skala regiment rejects accusations it sent soldiers to their deaths. 

How much territory Russia captured in March according to three sources. 

Russian authorities claim they have taken all of Luhansk region – for the third time. 

Charts of the day: Equipment losses in March. 

Videos of the day: a drone hits a drone operator, a strike on the refinery in Ufa, a Russian upgrade for drones.

The Russian independent website Dossier Center published interesting information last week about Russia’s 27th Guards Division. It shows how high losses at the front may really be for Russian units. In some sub-units they may approach 100 percent.

The independent investigative website based its findings on a document that Russian captain Oleg Miller, a member of this division, took with him when he deserted from it in 2025. It is a complete list of all Russian soldiers who served in the unit from the beginning of 2024 to January 2025 – from the division commander to a musician in the military band. It includes the soldiers’ names, dates of birth, service numbers, postings and the date they joined the unit.

“Thanks to this document it is possible to determine what losses Russian army combat units suffered, who serves in them and under what circumstances, who was killed and who survived,” the website wrote. The journalists randomly checked selected names and the document appeared to be authentic.

The list contains 28,234 names of soldiers who were assigned to this unit at the time. Normally, a comparable division has roughly 12,000 to 14,000 soldiers, in other words about half as many.

According to the website, this suggests how heavy the unit’s losses were – so heavy that it effectively had to recruit another 14,000 soldiers.

The unit has existed since 2024 and, in April, it had only 8,000 personnel. At that time it consisted of four motorised rifle regiments (506th, 589th, 433rd, and 1435th), the 268th self-propelled artillery regiment, as well as smaller support units.

In April 2024, the division was deployed near Ocheretyne, later it took part in the assault on Pokrovsk. In February 2026, reports emerged that it was attacking north of this town. In two years of existence, it advanced by 50 km. As Dossier Center wrote, this is an ordinary division – “neither elite, nor hopeless”.

We do not know what losses the unit has suffered up to now, but the document makes it possible to determine its losses up to January 2025, that is, over its first year of existence. Officially, only 12 death reports are recorded next to soldiers’ names. In addition, however, 4,778 soldiers were listed as missing for more than one day. These can almost certainly be considered killed. The journalists again randomly checked around 100 names and did not find any evidence in a single case that the person in question was alive.

The document does not provide data on wounded soldiers. However, Dossier Center wrote that the number of seriously wounded was probably twice as high. Mediazona and the Russian service of the BBC, which track killed Russian soldiers, also work with a 2:1 ratio.

Statistics from Ukrainian unmanned systems forces indicate that the current ratio between wounded and killed Russians after drone strikes is 1:1, although in 2024 it may have been different.

This means that more than half of the soldiers in this division were killed or seriously wounded. According to the website, in 2024 the risk of death for members of the division was between 15 and 17 percent, and the risk of injury between 32 and 35 percent.

That, however, applies to the unit as a whole. The risk of death varies depending on which part of the division a soldier serves in. It is higher in assault units than in support elements or the command structure.

From the document, the website found that artillerymen or drone operators were relatively safe. The 268th self-propelled artillery regiment reported only one missing (that is, dead) soldier. Units of FPV (first-person view) drone operators in this division did not record any losses in 2024.

Those who died were mainly members of assault units, and newly recruited soldiers were sent precisely there.

“If you are a new soldier who has signed a contract, you will most likely be sent to the unit with the most acute manpower shortage. In the case of the 27th Division, it turns out that the best chance for a new recruit is to be assigned to the 433rd Guards Motorised Rifle Regiment. And the chance of death or of suffering a severe, disabling injury is not 15 or 30 percent. It is quickly approaching 100 percent,” the website wrote.

In the 433rd motorised rifle regiment, 6,857 personnel fought during the period in question, of whom about 5,000 were killed or wounded. Even in this regiment there are support units that are relatively safe, which means that the risk of death or injury for front-line soldiers is even higher than 75 percent.

Despite this, Russian president Vladimir Putin upgraded this regiment to ‘Guards’ status in January 2025, and its commander was promoted to colonel. In February 2025, Aleksandr Buriy, the commander of the entire 27th Division, was promoted to major general.

Skala rejects accusations that it is sending people into a meat grinder. The 425th Separate Assault Regiment Skala responded to a post by the defence minister’s adviser, Serhiy Sternenko, in which he suggested that it was sending its troops into assault as recklessly as the Russians. The action took place on Tuesday west of Pokrovsk near the village of Hryshyne, as we reported on Wednesday.

Skala wrote on social media that Sternenko relied on videos from Russian sources that spliced together several attacks from different days. The regiment stated that on Tuesday it carried out “a successful counterattack on one of the key tactical sites in Pokrovsk that had been seized by the enemy”.

The unit claimed it had taken advantage of favourable weather conditions, attacked in well-armoured vehicles, and maintained proper communication with its command.

According to Skala, Russian drones hit four of its vehicles near the target. The DeepState UA account wrote that one Abrams and two Soviet BMP‑2s were destroyed.

According to Skala, a total of two soldiers in the vehicles were killed, while the rest of the crews were evacuated in a fifth vehicle that withdrew to the rear. Sternenko had earlier written that “the footage shows many bodies of dead Ukrainian soldiers”.

At the same time, Skala said it had managed to dismount the infantry, which remained on site. “The assault group at the point of attack is engaged in fighting, is in contact with the command and is receiving maximum fire support,” it wrote.

Sternenko was subsequently invited to the regiment’s headquarters to be briefed about the attack. “However, we consider it unacceptable to publicly disseminate a statement by a representative of the defence ministry that is not based on communication with participants in the operation or on data from an internal review, but solely on videos and statements by the enemy. Such an approach does not help to establish the truth, it only plays into the enemy’s hands,” Skala said.

They also called out other critics in the same way, saying they were open to new people who could tell them how they should conduct assaults differently.

It is not entirely clear where the truth lies. The OSINT account Playfra analysed a video of the attack that showed radio contact with infantry consolidating the area where they had dismounted from the vehicles. Other footage showed an interview with the driver who evacuated the crews.

The account confirmed three lost vehicles (it refers to BTRs instead of BMP‑2s). “Some of the infantry survived, regrouped, and is now providing intelligence directly from the field, likely reinforcing existing but weakened positions in the landing area; some of the infantry was also successfully evacuated back to friendly lines, while the rest was destroyed,” Playfra wrote.

In any case, the Ukrainian command will investigate the attack. The 7th Rapid Reaction Corps of the Air Assault Forces wrote on Facebook that this would happen.

Skala had already been criticised in the past for suffering high losses during assaults. According to the UAlosses website, 81 soldiers from this unit were killed in 2025; no casualties have been recorded this year so far, but the data are often updated only with some delay.

Russian advances in March were slow, similar to the month before. According to data from the DeepState UA account, the Russians captured a total of 160 square kms of new Ukrainian territory, only slightly more than in February. Overall, this was their second weakest month since April last year. The chart shows a repeat of the slowdown in Russian advances seen a year ago.

French OSINT analyst Clément Molin published a chart showing that Russian advances in March were even slower than in February, marking the smallest gains since June 2024.

Russian accounts likewise show that Russia is struggling to advance. The account Slivochnyi kapriz wrote that the area held by Russian forces increased by 166 square kms. According to this account as well, this is the smallest increase since July 2024.

As DeepState UA wrote, the number of Russian attacks fell by 2 percent compared with February, a negligible difference. It added, however, that these attacks were larger and of higher quality than before.

Two-thirds of all Russian operations are taking place in three sectors: Pokrovsk (29 percent), the Kostyantynivka area (20 percent), and Huliaipole (19 percent). Russia recorded its biggest advances around Huliaipole and Sloviansk.

If they continued to advance in Donetsk region at this pace, they would capture it in 2029, according to the account. Russia’s declared goal, however, is to seize the whole Donbas already this year.

“It is a numbers game, but there is a grain of truth in it: for example, to achieve their declared goal of seizing the region by the end of 2026, they would have to capture more than 600 square kms of Donetsk region every month – such a pace has been reached only once in recent years, during the most intense period in November 2024,” the DeepState UA account wrote.

On Monday, Russia’s defence ministry announced that it had captured the entire Luhansk region of Ukraine. But, as the Institute for the Study of War (ISW) wrote, this was not true, since Russia still did not have firm control over two villages in the region: Nadiya and Novoiehorivka. It thus held 99.84 percent of the region.

The DeepState UA account’s map shows that Ukrainians still hold two small pockets of territory.

As ISW wrote, this was the third time Russia had announced it had seized the area. The first time was in July 2022, which was true, but Ukrainians pushed them out of part of the territory during the autumn offensive later that year. The second time was in June 2025, when the head of the so‑called Luhansk People’s Republic, Leonid Pasechnik, made the claim, which, however, did not correspond to reality.

Videos of the day

Neutralising drone operators is becoming a priority for Ukrainian unmanned systems. Defence minister Mykhailo Fedorov announced that soldiers would receive double the number of points in the system used to allocate drones for doing so.

The video shows an FPV drone entering a destroyed hall and striking a Russian soldier, probably a drone operator.

Another successful Ukrainian strike on a Russian refinery. This time they hit the refinery in Ufa, about 1,400 km from the Ukrainian border. According to the Ukrainian account KyberBorošno, the footage suggests there was an intense fire in the area of the AVT‑5 unit, a primary oil‑processing installation. Ukraine also struck this refinery in October last year.

Russia is testing a device that can drop up to six munitions from a drone. It works similarly to a revolver cylinder: it rotates and small grenades fall out of it. As drone specialist Samuel Bendett wrote, it can be mounted on both Mavic and FPV drones.

What the losses are

On Thursday there is no update.

By Monday morning, Russia had verifiably lost 24,383 pieces of heavy equipment (on Monday 23 March it was 24,333). Of these, 19,028 (18,975) pieces were destroyed by Ukrainians, 971 (969) were damaged, 1,204 (1,210) were abandoned by their crews and 3,180 (3,179) were captured by the Ukrainian army. This includes 4,371 (4,365) tanks, of which 3,276 (3,268) were destroyed in combat. 

Ukraine has lost 11,697 (11,697) pieces of equipment, of which 9,027 (8,973) were destroyed, 656 (654) damaged, 661 (658) abandoned and 1,405 (1,412) captured. This includes 1,401 (1,401) tanks, of which 1,071 (1,067) were destroyed in combat.

Note: Neither side regularly reports its own dead or destroyed equipment. Ukraine publishes daily figures for Russian casualties and destroyed equipment, which cannot be independently verified. For this overview we use data from the Oryx project, which since the start of the war has compiled a list of equipment losses documented exclusively by photographs.
news.author: Tomáš Vasilko

news.title: Scientologists celebrate L. Ron Hubbard Birthday in 2026
news.text:
KINGNEWSWIRE // PRESS RELEASE // BRUSSELS, Belgium — April 2, 2026 — The 2026 celebration of the birthday of Scientology Founder L. Ron Hubbard brought together more than 5,000 Scientologists from 74 countries at Ruth Eckerd Hall in Clearwater, Florida, on March 14. The event combined historical milestones, organizational growth and a strong emphasis on passing Scientology principles to the next generation. Among the central announcements were the presentation of a newly restored L. Ron Hubbard Landmark Site in La Estrella, Telde, Canary Islands, and the release of new illustrated Scientology children’s books and related courses now available in 25 languages.

For Europe, the presentation of the restored villa in La Estrella carried particular significance. The property, perched above the Atlantic on Telde, Gran Canaria, holds a distinct place in Scientology history. It was there in 1967 that L. Ron Hubbard advanced work on upper spiritual levels and announced the creation of the Sea Organization, the Church’s religious order formed that same year to assist with advanced research operations and supervise Church organizations around the world.

The restored La Estrella property was presented as the tenth L. Ron Hubbard Landmark Site worldwide. The villa has been restored to reflect how it appeared during Hubbard’s stay, preserving the atmosphere and physical setting of that period. Among the items associated with the site are the Aiwa recorder used for Ron’s Journal 1967, the British Mark V E-Meter connected to Hubbard’s research into advanced spiritual levels, a 16 mm motion picture camera and communications equipment used while working from the Atlantic location. The site extends the international network of places tied to the formative development of Dianetics and Scientology.

The presentation of La Estrella added a strong European dimension to an event that also reviewed broader developments across Scientology’s international network. The evening highlighted new executive teams returning to churches in North America, Europe and Australia after advanced training, with Padova in northern Italy among the locations specifically featured. The program also recognized the newly opened Ideal Church in San Juan, Puerto Rico, as part of the continuing expansion long associated with L. Ron Hubbard birthday commemorations.

The second major development of the evening focused on children’s education. Mr David Miscavige, ecclesiastical leader of the Scientology religion, introduced a new library of illustrated Scientology books designed to present the religion’s basic principles in an accessible way for younger readers and families. The new release includes two 10-volume sets: one for children from age seven and another for more advanced readers. All 20 books, together with their corresponding courses, are now available in 25 languages, forming a worldwide curriculum for the next generation.

The educational initiative was presented not as a side project, but as a structural development across Scientology Churches. Dedicated children’s course rooms have now been established in all 79 Ideal Churches worldwide, providing spaces designed specifically for young Scientologists. The announcement gave the evening a notably intergenerational character, linking preservation of heritage with the transmission of beliefs, values and study materials to children and adolescents, and everyone else, in a format tailored to their age.

The celebration also revisited the wider legacy of L. Ron Hubbard as an author, educator and founder whose work extended beyond theology into ethical and social programs. The event noted his recognition in January 2026 by the Independent Book Publishers Association as “Master of All Genres” and highlighted the continuing reach of initiatives inspired by his writings, including study methods, drug prevention and The Way to Happiness, described during the celebration as having surpassed 145 million copies distributed worldwide.

Founded in 1952, Scientology has long marked L. Ron Hubbard’s birthday as a moment to review progress, new projects and milestones in the religion’s development. This year’s celebration stood out for joining two themes that resonate strongly in Europe: historical preservation and responsibility toward future generations. In that sense, the restored site in the Canary Islands and the new children’s educational materials formed two parts of the same message — continuity anchored in heritage and sustained through learning.

Ivan Arjona, representative of the Church of Scientology to the EU, OSCE, Council of Europe and UN, said the 2026 celebration reflected a connection between Europe’s cultural heritage and the responsibility to transmit values responsibly to younger generations. “The presentation of the restored La Estrella landmark in the Canary Islands is meaningful not only for Scientologists, but also in a broader European sense and even the very people of the Canary Islands and specifically Telde, because it shows the importance of preserving places where ideas, decisions and history were shaped,” Arjona said. “At the same time, making core principles understandable to children in many languages reflects an enduring civic value in Europe: that a tradition remains alive when it can be studied, understood and carried forward by the next generation.”

The 2026 birthday celebration therefore unfolded not simply as a commemorative event, but as a statement of continuity. With a newly restored landmark in Spain, a visible European footprint in the program and a major educational initiative aimed at younger generations across many languages, the event presented Scientology’s past, present and future as part of one connected narrative.

The Church of Scientology, its churches, missions, groups and members are present across the European continent. Scientology Europe reports a continent-wide presence through more than 140 churches, missions and affiliated groups in at least 27 European nations, alongside thousands of community-based social betterment and reform initiatives focused on education, prevention and neighbourhood-level support, inspired by the work of Scientology founder L. Ron Hubbard.

Within Europe’s diverse national frameworks for religion, the Church’s recognitions continue to expand, with administrative and judicial authorities in Spain, Portugal, Sweden, the Netherlands, Italy, Germany Slovakia and others, as well as the European Court of Human Rights, having addressed and acknowledged Scientology communities as protected by the national and international provisions of Freedom of Religion or belief.
news.summary: KINGNEWSWIRE // PRESS RELEASE // BRUSSELS, Belgium — April 2, 2026 — The 2026 celebration of the birthday of Scientology Founder L. Ron Hubbard brought together more than 5,000 Scientologists from 74 countries at Ruth Eckerd Hall in Clearwater, Florida, on March 14. The event combined historical milestones, organizational growth and a strong emphasis on passing Scientology principles […]
news.author: Newsdesk

news.title: Singular and Plural for the Holy Trinity in Holy Scripture
news.text:
By Nikolaos Sotiropoulo

Even in the Old Testament, God gave a foretaste of His trinity

According to us, the Orthodox, but also according to Roman Catholics and almost all Protestants, God is three Persons: the Father, who is unbegotten, the Son, who is born directly from the Father, and the Holy Spirit, who proceeds directly from the Father. An example, to get some idea of ​​the mystery, is the sun. And it is three things: the disk, the light, which is born directly from the disk, and the heat, which proceeds directly from the disk. The disk is the sun, the light is the sun, the heat is the sun, but there are not three suns, but one sun. So too, God is the Father, God is the Son, God is the Holy Spirit, but there are not three Gods, but one God. Because all three persons have the same essence – the Godhead.

The adherents of chiliasm (a doctrine of the 1000-year earthly kingdom of Christ, which is professed today by Jehovah’s Witnesses) deny this greatest truth of our faith, namely that the one in essence God is triune in hypostases or persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. According to them, Yahweh is only the Father, and the Son is outside the divine essence – a creation of the Father, not begotten; and the Holy Spirit is simply a force, not a person.

Many paradoxical and amazing places in Scripture, unthinkable and inaccessible to the human mind, hint at or clearly reveal the mystery of the triune God. In the following exposition we indicate some of them.

Mysterious plurals

“And God said, Let us make man in our image, after our likeness… And God created man; in the image of God created he him” (Gen. 1:26–27).

God did not say, “Let me make man in my image,” but said in the plural, “Let us make man in our image.” In the sense of this plural, it is excluded that angels should be included, because “God created” man, not angels, and created him “in the image of God,” not in the image of the angels. Therefore, God is not one single person, but several who jointly participated in the creation of man.

“And the Lord God said, Behold, Adam has become as one of Us” (Gen. 3:22).

The expression “one of us” excludes the possibility that “us” is a plural of greatness. That is, God said, “Behold, Adam has become as one of Us, knowing good and evil—the divine persons.”

“And the Lord said… Come, let us go down, and there confuse their language… For there the Lord confused the language of all the earth” (Gen. 11:6–9). The form in the 2nd l. pl. The verb “come” (in the synodal translation of the Bulgarian language: “let us descend”, note p.) excludes the use of the plural of greatness. Also, “come” shows that God (the Father) is addressing more than one divine person.

“And I heard the voice of the Lord, saying, Whom shall I send, and who will go for Us?” (Isa. 6:8, according to the Masoretic text). The Lord speaks in the singular of Himself (“to send”) and in the plural (“for us”), because He also has in mind the other divine persons.

“Present your work, saith the Lord… let them bring it forth, and tell us what is to come; let them shew a thing before it be, that we may consider it with our minds, and know the end thereof; or let them foretell us the things to come. Tell us what is to come hereafter, that we may know that ye are gods; or do good, or do evil, that we may wonder, and see with you. … Who hath declared this from the beginning, that we may know? And from long ago, that we may say…” (Isa. 41:21–23, 26 according to the Masoretic text; see also ch. 9). “Who among them hath declared this, and shewed us former things?” (Isa. 43:9 according to the Masoretic text). In these two passages God uses multiple plural forms of Himself simultaneously.

“Thus says the Lord: We have heard a voice of trouble and fear” (Jer. 30:5, according to the Masoretic text). The Lord says “we have heard” because it is more than one person.

“Thus says the Lord God concerning Edom: We have heard a report from the Lord” (Obadiah 1, according to the Masoretic text). The Lord says “we have heard from the Lord” because more than one person, but one in essence the Lord, heard from another Lord, evidently from the Father.

“Jesus answered him and said… Verily, verily, I say unto you, We speak that which we know, and testify that which we have seen; and ye receive not our testimony” (John 3:10–11). Here Jesus speaks in the plural, because besides Himself He also has in mind the Holy Spirit (cf. John 3:5–8, 34).

“I and the Father are one” (John 10:30). By the plural form “we” the Son associates Himself with the Father. It is noteworthy that He mentions Himself first and then the Father. A man or an angel would never say, “I and the Father are one.” The Son and the Father are two Persons, but one essence—one Godhead.

“Jesus answered and said unto him, If a man love me, he will keep my word: and my Father will love him, and we will come unto him, and make our abode with him” (John 14:23). Jesus and his Father come and dwell in every believer, which signifies their omnipresence and therefore their divinity.

“Holy Father, keep them in thine own name, which thou hast given me; that they may be one, even as we are” (John 17:11). “As thou, Father, art in me, and I in thee, that they also may be one in us… And the glory which thou gavest me I have given them, that they may be one, even as we are one” (John 17:12–22). Here Jesus identifies himself with God the Father and says “we,” “us,” and “are one”—something that no man or angel would ever do.

Particularly noteworthy are two similar, highly paradoxical plurals in two passages of the Hebrew text of the Old Testament. We quote the passages exactly as they are in the Hebrew text, not in translations that change these plurals to singulars.

“No one says, ‘Where is God my Maker, who gives songs in the night?’” (Job 35:10, וְלֹ֣א אָמְר֔וּ אַיֵּ֖ה אֱלֹ֣והַּ עֹשָׂ֑י נֹתֵ֖ן זְמִיר֣וֹת בַּלָּֽיְלָה׃, Masoretic Text).

“Remember your Creator in the days of your youth” (Eccl. 12:1, וּזְכֹ֣ר אֶת־בּוֹרְאֶ֔יךָ בִּימֵ֖י בְחוּרוֹתֶ֑יךָ עַ֚ד אֲשֶׁ֣ר לֹא־יָבֹ֔אוּ יְמֵ֖י הָרָעָ֑ה וְהִגִּ֥יעוּ שָׁנִ֖ים אֲשֶׁר־תֹּאמַ֥ר אֵין־לִ֖י בָהֶֽם־חֵֽפֶץ׃).

So, it does not say “God is my Creator”, but “God is my Creators”. Nor does it say “Remember your Creator”, but “Remember your Creators”.

The one God in essence is more than one Person – Creators or Makers of man (cf. Gen. 1:26).

The mysterious singular numbers

“Let us make man in our image” (Gen. 1:26). There are many divine persons, but one image (one essence).

“Now may our God and Father himself, and our Lord Jesus Christ, direct our way to you” (1 Thess. 3:11). Since it is about two – God and Christ, the apostle should have used the plural: “to direct”. But the apostle used the singular “to direct”, because the two are one in essence – one “Lord”, as it is said in the next verse (12).

“Now may our Lord Jesus Christ himself, and God and our Father, who loved us… comfort your hearts and establish you in every word and good deed” (2 Thess. 2:16–17). Here too, since it is about two – Christ and God, the apostle should have used the plural: “to comfort”, “to strengthen”. But he used the singulars “to comfort,” “to strengthen,” because the two are one in essence.

“And the kingdoms of the world have become the kingdoms of our Lord and of His Christ, and He will reign forever and ever” (Rev. 11:15). Since the kingdoms pass to two—the Lord and the Christ—it would be normal to say “will reign.” But the singular “will reign” is used, because the two are one in essence, the King.

“They will be priests of God and of Christ, and will reign with Him” (Rev. 20:6). It would be normal to say “and will reign with them.” But it is said “and will reign with Him,” because God and Christ are one in essence.

“And the throne of God and of the Lamb will be in the city, and His servants will serve Him; and they will see His face, and His name will be on their foreheads” (Rev. 22:3–4). This passage is extremely paradoxical and amazing: there are many singulars where plurals are usually expected. That is, the passage should normally read: “And the thrones of God and of the Lamb will be in the city. And their servants will serve them; and they will see their faces, and their names will be on their foreheads.” Through the many singulars in this passage from Revelation, the essential unity of God and the Lamb is shown in a way that we can call classical. God and the Lamb have one throne, one image, one name! And believers in God and the Lamb are servants of One and serve Him as One! We should pay special attention to the fact that God and the Lamb have one name.

Excerpt from the book: Αντιχιλιαστικόν ἐγχειρίδιον (Antimillennial handbook); 5th edition, Athens 1994.
news.summary: By Nikolaos Sotiropoulo Even in the Old Testament, God gave a foretaste of His trinity According to us, the Orthodox, but also according to Roman Catholics and almost all Protestants, God is three Persons: the Father, who is unbegotten, the Son, who is born directly from the Father, and the Holy Spirit, who proceeds directly […]
news.author: Guest Author

news.title: 10 Interesting Facts About Ladybugs
news.text:
There are about 5,000 different species of ladybugs in the world. They differ mainly in their colors and spots – there are 2-spotted, 10-spotted, 14-spotted and even 24-spotted. The most popular and common are those with a shiny, red body with seven black spots.

The number, shape and arrangement of the spots are determined by the type of ladybug and are formed while the insect is still emerging from its pupa. There are species that are yellow with black spots, and those that are black with red spots.

Their sizes vary from four to ten millimeters. They have an elongated, oval or round body with a strongly convex back. The surface of some species of these insects is covered with thin hairs. The body consists of a small head, thorax, abdomen and wings with elytra. In addition, each individual has three pairs of legs.

Representatives of almost all species are considered predators. The basis of their diet is mites and aphids. But they do not neglect the larvae of other insects, butterfly eggs and small caterpillars. In nature, there are also species that feed exclusively on plants, flowers, leaves, pollen and even mushroom mycelium.

Ladybugs are also cannibals. If food is scarce, they resort to cannibalism – a hungry ladybug, without hesitation, would eat its own kind in order to survive.

Ladybugs have good protection against most of their enemies. Their red-black color warns predators that they will regret it if they try them. When a bird or other animal tries to attack it, the ladybug releases droplets of poisonous blood from the joints of its legs. It contains cantharidin.

This poisonous, unpleasant-smelling yellow liquid discourages birds and other potential enemies. The attacker tastes the bitter blood and spits out the ladybug. Predators learn to interpret the red-and-black color of the wings as a message to stay away.

The highest speed that a ladybug can reach is 24 kilometers per hour. The number of times its wings flap can reach an enviable 85 per second. It can fly 120 kilometers without stopping at the speed of a galloping horse.

The red bug can stay in the air for two hours and reach a fairly high altitude of 1,200 meters.

The way in which a ladybug retracts its wings after a flight is also interesting. This beetle carefully folds its delicate wings under its red-and-black elytra (elytra) when it lands. The insect makes a complex series of movements, resembling folding origami, to hide its wings.

Its wings are both strong enough to support flight, and yet so flexible. The shape of the veins on its wings allows it to roll up like a roller, making them stiff but foldable.

All ladybugs lead a solitary lifestyle. They only come together during hibernation or during the mating season. This process begins in early March, and sometimes the pairing of the insects lasts up to 9 hours in a row.

There can be a maximum of 5-6 generations of ladybugs in a year, with the average time from egg to adult ladybug taking only about 3-4 weeks. Females lay between 50 and 300 eggs. The small eggs are yellow and oval in shape and are usually found in groups of 10-50.

Ladybugs lay eggs on the underside of plant leaves, where aphids usually gather so that the small larvae can feed. The larvae, which vary in shape and color, emerge within a few days after the eggs are laid.

They grow extremely quickly and shed their skin several times. Then they attach themselves to a leaf with a tail-like structure and turn into pupae. Within a week or two, the pupae turn into ladybugs.

Ladybugs are most active from spring to autumn. When the weather gets cold, they find a warm, secluded place where they wait for the weather to warm up again.

Representatives of some species meet the winter in countries with a more favorable climate, while others remain to wait out the cold, merging into numerous groups consisting of several million. Such places are most often in hollows in trees, under stones and even in people’s houses.

In 1999, the American space agency NASA sent 4 ladybugs and aphids into space to test their behavior in airless space. The ladybugs that were on board were named after the four members of the Beatles – John, Paul, Ringo and George. All of them survived the space flight, even feeding on aphids in the microgravity environment that was unusual for them.

Illustrative Photo by Pixabay: https://www.pexels.com/photo/2-lady-bug-on-green-leaf-144243

The most popular and common are those with a shiny, red body with seven black spots. The number, shape and arrangement of the spots are determined by […]
news.author: Gaston de Persigny

news.title: What awaits Kazakhstan after the adoption of a new constitution
news.text:
Kazakhstan has taken a significant step towards strengthening its ability to operate effectively in a complex and rapidly changing domestic and international environment

In mid-March, the citizens of the largest country in Central Asia voted for a new constitution. 87% of voters voted for the main state document, which indicates the unconditional support of Kazakhstanis for the course of President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev.

The Tokayev Constitution is a completely new law (the changes affect 77 articles, which is more than 80% of the text), and in this situation it is really interesting to understand what exactly is changing in the Central Asian republic for its further development. In this regard, I would like to note for Bulgarian readers a number of fundamental positions that are close to the South Slavs.

First of all, the Constitution of Kazakhstan included for the first time an article: “Marriage is a voluntary and equal union of a man and a woman, registered by the state in accordance with the law.” It is obvious that the leader of Kazakhstan, Kassym-Jomart Tokayev, who proposed such a formulation, advocates traditional family values, which, of course, brings Kazakhs closer to Bulgarians, who are not so inclined to accept “gender diversity” in the European Union. Kazakhs, like Bulgarians, are for normal, God-given human relationships, concerned about future generations.

The second innovation: the previously established norm that international treaties are higher than national laws was removed from the constitution. Thus, Kazakhstan proclaims the supremacy of national law over that imposed from the outside, which looks very nice from the EU, given the total legal dictate from Brussels. In addition, Kazakhstan has tightened the rules for foreign financing of political parties. Now foreign companies, enterprises with foreign participation and stateless persons have been added to the list of prohibited sources of financing.

Thirdly, clauses on the secular nature of education and, in general, on the separation of religion from the state have appeared in the constitution. Thus, the largest Muslim country is resolutely fighting radical Islam, realizing the critical danger from religious fanatics (from the southern border of Kazakhstan to Afghanistan – less than a thousand kilometers of mostly mountainous terrain, very difficult for border control). It is no secret that interreligious conflicts are a favorite dish on the “menu” of geopolitical opponents, especially when a given state is a multi-religious construct. And it is completely reasonable for Kazakhstan to lay down in its basic law a secular model of development (with multi-religious synthesis, of course). Of course, Bulgarians are impressed by this approach in the spiritual sphere.

Finally, it should be noted that Kazakhstan has retained in its basic law the status of the Russian language as an official language, and the state language, Kazakh, is based on the Cyrillic alphabet, which is similar to the Bulgarian alphabet. In addition, the Constitution of Kazakhstan protects the right of everyone to speak their native language, and those who dare to discriminate against people on linguistic (as well as national) grounds will be held accountable by law.

Illustrative Photo by Aibek Skakov: https://www.pexels.com/photo/kazakhstan-national-flag-waving-against-sky-36506361

news.author: Newsroom

news.title: Scientists have revealed the link between high IQ and late bedtime
news.text:
People who spend too much time in bed are at higher risk of mortality.

If you regularly fall asleep after midnight, it may not be just a bad habit. According to scientists from the University of Southampton, going to bed late may be associated with a higher level of intelligence.

A study involving more than 1,200 people shows that people with an IQ of over 125 are more likely to go to bed after midnight and wake up later than others. The results, published in the National Library of Medicine, reveal an interesting relationship: “night owls” not only have higher IQs, but they also often enjoy higher incomes, as well as higher life satisfaction.

Of course, scientists warn that the cause-and-effect relationship is not entirely clear. It is possible that people with higher incomes simply have more freedom to determine their own schedule, without being forced to get up early.

Despite these curious findings, experts are categorical – a nightly schedule is not necessarily healthy.

Another study, conducted by the University of Birmingham and published in the Journal of the American Heart Association, shows that falling asleep after 2 hours at night increases the risk of cardiovascular disease by about 16%.

In addition, people who spend too much time in bed (over 12 hours) are at a higher risk of mortality compared to those who sleep about 7–8 hours.

Being a “night owl” does not necessarily mean a problem – and may even be associated with certain advantages. But this is not a reason for pride, nor an excuse for an unhealthy schedule.

The most important thing remains balance – enough sleep, tailored to the needs of the body, regardless of whether you go to bed early or late.

Illustrative Photo by Polina: https://www.pexels.com/photo/a-woman-sleeping-wearing-a-sleep-mask-6541206

According to scientists from the University of Southampton, going to bed late may be associated with a higher level of intelligence. A study involving more than […]

news.title: Switzerland’s President Advocates for Wine Import Restrictions Amidst Controversy
news.text:
Based on reporting by Europe.

Switzerland’s president, who is also involved in wine production, is advocating for restrictions on wine imports. This initiative aims to support local wine producers but has faced backlash from foreign competitors and domestic merchants concerned about potential economic impacts.

The president’s proposal seeks to strengthen Switzerland’s domestic wine industry. The goal is to provide Swiss winemakers with a competitive advantage in a market where they face competition from foreign imports. The president argues that limiting imports could help local producers thrive by reducing competition from often cheaper international wines.

However, this stance has met resistance. Foreign wine exporters argue that such measures could lead to retaliatory trade barriers and disrupt the free flow of goods. Swiss merchants dealing in imported wines express concerns over potential revenue losses and reduced consumer choice.

The controversy highlights the tension between supporting local industries and maintaining free trade principles. Proponents emphasize the importance of preserving traditional Swiss winemaking techniques and sustaining rural economies reliant on viticulture.

Critics warn that protectionism could isolate Switzerland economically and harm its reputation as an open market economy. They fear that similar measures in other sectors could lead to inward-looking policies that stifle innovation and growth.

Economically, restricting wine imports could benefit local producers by reducing competition, potentially increasing market share and prices for Swiss wines. This might lead to more investment in local vineyards and job creation within the sector.

Conversely, consumers might face higher prices and fewer choices. Importers and retailers dependent on foreign wines could see declines in sales, impacting profitability and possibly leading to job losses. There are concerns about how such restrictions might affect Switzerland’s broader trade relationships.

The future of Switzerland’s wine import policy remains uncertain as discussions continue. The government must balance the benefits of protecting its domestic industry against the risks of alienating trade partners and disrupting economic relationships.

Stakeholders are closely watching the negotiations unfold. This debate reflects larger global discussions about balancing national interests with international cooperation—a challenge faced by many countries today.

Original source: Europe

Image prompt (copy/paste)
news.summary: Switzerland's president, a wine producer, is proposing restrictions on wine imports to bolster local production. This move has sparked opposition from international competitors and domestic merchants who fear economic repercussions. The debate highlights tensions between supporting local industries and maintaining open trade.
news.author: Newsdesk

news.title: Do cats really heal?
news.text:
Cat owners have often noticed that when a person has a headache, stomach ache or joint pain, their fluffy pet often lies down in the affected area.

Is this just a coincidence? Veterinarians offer several explanations for this phenomenon.

The first is the thermal effect: a cat’s body temperature is 38-39°C, which is higher than a human’s. The heat improves blood circulation and can relieve muscle and joint pain.

The second is the vibrations from the purr: the frequency of a cat’s purr (20-150 Hz) corresponds to the therapeutic frequencies used in physiotherapy to promote tissue healing, reduce pain and inflammation.

The third is emotional support: contact with a cat reduces stress, anxiety and blood pressure, which contributes to an overall improvement in well-being. 6699

And as a fourth explanation, there is a strong sensitivity to change: cats have an exceptional sense of smell and can detect chemical changes in the human body associated with illness or inflammation. And what do scientists say?

A study conducted at the Animal Communication Institute in North Carolina found that cat owners are 30% less likely to seek medical help for minor ailments. Another study published in the Journal of Vascular and Interventional Neurology found that people who have a pet cat have a reduced risk of death from heart attacks and strokes.

It is also important to remember that cats are not a substitute for professional medical care, but they can be a wonderful addition to treatment, a source of emotional support and a natural “therapist” in both the literal and figurative sense.

They really have the ability to identify problem areas in the human body and instinctively seek to help. Their therapeutic effect is a combination of physiological factors (warmth, vibrations) and powerful psychological support.

Perhaps one of the reasons for the millennia-old bond between humans and cats lies precisely in this amazing ability to sense and relieve their owner’s pain. So the next time your pet lies down on a sore spot, thank him – he really is trying to help you heal.

Illustrative Photo by Akbar Nemati: https://www.pexels.com/photo/cute-tabby-and-ginger-cat-5622738
